subtopic: Heavy menstrual bleeding - structured PALM-COEIN workup
stem: A 44-year-old G2P2 woman attends her GP with an 8-month history of progressively heavier menstrual bleeding. Cycles are regular at 28 days but bleeding now lasts 9 days with flooding through super tampons hourly on days 2-3 and passage of large clots up to 3 cm in size. She reports increasing tiredness, palpitations on minor exertion, and exertional shortness of breath climbing one flight of stairs. Her work as a primary school teacher has been disrupted and she has had to change pads during teaching sessions. She is not currently sexually active, has had no postcoital bleeding, no intermenstrual bleeding, and no postcoital spotting. There is no abdominal or pelvic pain. There is no easy bruising, no gum bleeding, no epistaxis, no family bleeding history and her last cervical screening test was negative for oncogenic HPV 18 months ago. BMI is 34. She has hypertension treated with perindopril 5 mg daily and takes no anticoagulants, no antiplatelets and no over-the-counter NSAIDs. She has had two normal vaginal deliveries with no postpartum haemorrhage. She has not yet had pelvic imaging or endometrial sampling in this presentation.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/86 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, T 36.6 degC.
data_table.Examination: Pale conjunctivae. Abdomen soft, no palpable mass. Bimanual: uterus bulky, mobile, anteverted, no adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 92 g/L (115-165), MCV 74 fL, ferritin 6 mcg/L. Beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: In addition to a transvaginal ultrasound and full blood count with ferritin, which further investigation is most important at this visit?

options.text: FSH and oestradiol to assess for perimenopausal anovulation
options.rationale: Random FSH/oestradiol in a 44-year-old with regular cycles is unhelpful; cycle regularity already argues against anovulation and hormonal levels fluctuate widely in the perimenopause (RANZCOG HMB).

options.text: Coagulation screen and von Willebrand panel as the first-line bleeding work-up
options.rationale: Inherited bleeding disorders are screened when HMB has been present since menarche or there are personal/family bleeding cues; her 8-month history and absence of bleeding red flags do not prioritise this (RANZCOG; NICE NG88).

options.text: Endometrial biopsy (Pipelle) at this visit, alongside TSH and coagulation screen as adjuncts
options.rationale: RANZCOG and NICE NG88 recommend endometrial sampling in women with HMB who are aged 45 or over OR who have risk factors for endometrial hyperplasia/cancer; obesity (BMI 34) is a major risk factor and TVUS alone is insufficient to exclude endometrial pathology in this setting (RANZCOG; NICE NG88).

options.text: Hysteroscopy with directed biopsy as the sole next investigation in place of TVUS
options.rationale: Hysteroscopy is appropriate where TVUS suggests focal pathology or sampling is unsuccessful, but TVUS remains the recommended first-line imaging in HMB (NICE NG88).

options.text: CA-125 to screen for ovarian malignancy
options.rationale: CA-125 is not part of HMB work-up; it is non-specific in premenopausal women and has no role here (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Workup of HMB follows PALM-COEIN. TVUS plus FBC/ferritin are universal. Endometrial sampling is indicated at age >=45, persistent intermenstrual bleeding, failed medical therapy, or risk factors for endometrial cancer (obesity, anovulation, tamoxifen, Lynch). TSH and coagulation screen are adjuncts based on history.
key_points: PALM (structural): Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy/hyperplasia
COEIN (non-structural): Coagulopathy, Ovulatory, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified
FBC + ferritin in all; TSH if cycle change or clinical features; coags if bleeding history since menarche
Biopsy if age >=45 OR risk factor for endometrial cancer (obesity, PCOS, tamoxifen, Lynch, unopposed oestrogen)
TVUS first-line imaging; saline-infusion sonohysterography or hysteroscopy for focal lesions
explanation.pearls: Obesity lowers the age threshold for endometrial sampling. Don't anchor on age 45 if other risk factors are present.

subtopic: Uterine fibroids - choice of medical therapy
stem: A 39-year-old G1P1 woman has a 2-year history of heavy regular menstrual bleeding with cycle-related bulk symptoms including urinary frequency, mild lower-abdominal pressure, and occasional nocturia. She passes large clots during menses and floods through super tampons every 1-2 hours on the heaviest days. Pelvic ultrasound shows a 4 cm intramural fibroid (FIGO type 4, not distorting the cavity) and a 3 cm subserosal fibroid; the endometrial cavity is regular and the endometrium is 6 mm. Endometrial biopsy returns benign proliferative endometrium with no atypia. She has completed her family, is in a monogamous relationship using condoms for contraception, and would prefer to avoid surgery if possible. She does not want to lose her fertility surgically and is open to a long-acting hormonal device. She has tried tranexamic acid 1 g three times daily during menses with only modest improvement and has not tolerated mefenamic acid. BMI 26. She has no contraindications to oestrogen or progestogen, no migraines and no history of VTE.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, lower-uterine mass palpable just above pubis. Bimanual: bulky irregular uterus approx 12-week size, mobile.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 101 g/L (115-165), ferritin 14 mcg/L. TVUS as above. Beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line medical therapy?

options.text: GnRH agonist (e.g. goserelin 3.6 mg SC monthly) with add-back oestrogen
options.rationale: GnRH analogues are used short-term (3-6 months) to shrink fibroids prior to surgery or to bridge perimenopause; they are not first-line ongoing therapy due to vasomotor symptoms and bone-density loss (RANZCOG; NICE NG88).

options.text: Levonorgestrel 52 mg intrauterine system (Mirena)
options.rationale: The LNG-IUS is first-line medical therapy for HMB including fibroid-related HMB where the uterine cavity is not distorted by submucosal fibroids; it reduces blood loss by 70-95% and is acceptable for a woman who has completed her family (RANZCOG; NICE NG88).

options.text: Tranexamic acid 1 g orally three times daily during menses only
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid reduces blood loss by 40-50% during menses but does not address bulk symptoms and is a less effective overall option than the LNG-IUS for HMB (NICE NG88).

options.text: Mefenamic acid 500 mg orally three times daily during menses only
options.rationale: NSAIDs reduce blood loss by ~25-30% and help dysmenorrhoea; they are useful adjuncts but not first-line for moderate-severe HMB with anaemia and bulk symptoms (NICE NG88).

options.text: Total abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral salpingectomy
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is definitive but should be reserved for women who have failed or declined effective medical therapy; this patient prefers to avoid surgery and has not yet trialled a LARC (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: For fibroid-related HMB without significant cavity distortion: LNG-IUS first-line. Tranexamic acid and NSAIDs are useful adjuncts. GnRH analogues are short-term pre-surgical or perimenopausal bridging agents. Myomectomy preserves fertility; hysterectomy is definitive when family complete and other options failed.
key_points: LNG-IUS: first-line for HMB without cavity distortion (FIGO types 3-7 generally OK)
Submucosal fibroids (FIGO 0-2) distorting cavity: hysteroscopic myomectomy
GnRH agonist: short-term shrinkage pre-op or perimenopausal bridge
Uterine artery embolisation: alternative when fertility not desired
Myomectomy: preserves fertility; hysterectomy: definitive cure
explanation.pearls: Mirena requires a cavity that the device can sit in. A large submucosal fibroid distorting the cavity contraindicates Mirena and instead triggers hysteroscopic myomectomy.

stem: A 27-year-old nulligravid woman presents with a 4-year history of cyclical pelvic pain that has worsened over the past 12 months. Pain begins 2 days before menses, peaks on day 1-2 of the cycle requiring time off work, and is associated with deep dyspareunia, pain on defecation during menses (dyschezia) and occasional cyclical lower-back pain radiating into the right thigh. She has tried regular ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily during menses with only partial benefit and a heat-pack regimen with minimal improvement. She is not pregnant, in a monogamous relationship using condoms, and is not currently planning conception in the next 18-24 months. Her last cervical screening test was normal 12 months ago. She has no urinary symptoms and no abnormal vaginal bleeding. Her menarche was age 13 and her cycles are regular at 28 days. BMI 24. She would prefer non-surgical management initially if possible and has no contraindications to combined hormonal contraception, no migraine with aura, no smoking and no VTE history.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, no masses. Bimanual: uterus anteverted, mobile, mildly tender. No palpable nodularity in the pouch of Douglas. No adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Pelvic USS: normal-appearing ovaries, no endometrioma, no deep infiltrating lesion visualised.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Diagnostic laparoscopy with peritoneal biopsy before any therapy
options.rationale: Empirical hormonal therapy is recommended before laparoscopy in women without red flags or imaging findings of deep disease; surgery is offered after failed medical therapy (ESHRE 2022; RANZCOG).

options.text: Serum CA-125 to support the diagnosis
options.rationale: CA-125 has poor sensitivity and specificity for endometriosis and is not recommended (ESHRE 2022).

options.text: MRI pelvis as the next investigation to confirm diagnosis
options.rationale: MRI is reserved for suspected deep infiltrating endometriosis, surgical planning, or where TVUS is inconclusive; it is not first-line and not required to commence empirical therapy (ESHRE 2022).

options.text: Trial of continuous combined oral contraceptive pill with NSAID for breakthrough pain
options.rationale: First-line empirical therapy for suspected endometriosis without contraindications is hormonal suppression (continuous COCP, POP or LNG-IUS) plus NSAID; effective in 70-80% (ESHRE 2022; RANZCOG).

options.text: GnRH agonist with add-back HRT as first-line therapy
options.rationale: GnRH analogues are second-line after COCP or progestogen failure due to vasomotor symptoms and bone-density loss (ESHRE 2022).

explanation.summary: Endometriosis diagnosis is clinical; TVUS often normal in superficial peritoneal disease. Laparoscopy is gold standard for visual/histological confirmation but not required to commence empirical therapy. First-line medical mgmt: continuous COCP/POP/LNG-IUS + NSAID.
key_points: Classic features: cyclical pelvic pain, dysmenorrhoea, deep dyspareunia, dyschezia, dysuria
Empirical hormonal therapy precedes laparoscopy in straightforward presentations
TVUS first-line imaging; MRI for suspected deep disease
Laparoscopy: failed medical therapy, suspected deep disease, infertility, severe symptoms
Refer to gynaecologist if no response in 3-6 months or red flags
explanation.pearls: Normal pelvic ultrasound does not exclude endometriosis. Trial hormonal therapy empirically.

subtopic: PCOS - Rotterdam criteria and AU management
stem: A 23-year-old nulligravid woman is reviewed for 14 months of oligomenorrhoea (cycles 50-90 days), worsening facial hirsutism requiring waxing every 2-3 weeks, and 7 kg weight gain over 12 months despite no major dietary change. She has not been sexually active in the last year and is not using any contraception currently. She is not planning pregnancy in the next 2-3 years and has no contraindications to combined hormonal contraception, no migraine with aura, no smoking and no personal or family history of VTE. She has mild acne over the chin and jawline that flares premenstrually. There is no galactorrhoea, no temperature intolerance, no rapidly progressive virilisation (no voice change, no clitoromegaly), no headaches and no visual symptoms. Family history: mother has type 2 diabetes diagnosed in her 50s. Investigations confirm biochemical hyperandrogenism (elevated free androgen index), normal TSH, normal prolactin, normal 17-hydroxyprogesterone and a negative beta-hCG. Pelvic USS shows 24 follicles per ovary and ovarian volume 11 mL bilaterally. Fasting glucose 5.4 mmol/L, HbA1c 5.6%. BMI 31. Modified Ferriman-Gallwey score 11.

data_table.Examination: BMI 31. Hirsutism upper lip, chin, chest. Acanthosis nigricans nape. No virilisation. BP 124/78 mmHg.
data_table.Investigations: TSH 1.8 mIU/L; prolactin normal; 17-OHP normal; total testosterone mildly elevated, SHBG low, FAI elevated; beta-hCG negative; TVUS as above; HbA1c 5.6%.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line management for menstrual regulation and androgenic symptoms?

options.text: Lifestyle intervention combined with a combined oral contraceptive pill
options.rationale: The 2023 international evidence-based PCOS guideline recommends lifestyle intervention as foundational for all women with PCOS, with the COCP as first-line pharmacological management for irregular cycles and hyperandrogenism in those not seeking pregnancy (Teede et al. 2023).

options.text: Spironolactone 100 mg daily monotherapy
options.rationale: Spironolactone is an effective anti-androgen but is used as an add-on to a reliable contraceptive (such as COCP) because it is teratogenic and does not regulate cycles (Teede et al. 2023).

options.text: Metformin 500 mg twice daily monotherapy without contraception
options.rationale: Metformin is an adjunct (particularly when BMI >25 or impaired glucose tolerance is present) and helps metabolic features and ovulation; it is not first-line for hirsutism or contraceptive cycle regulation (Teede et al. 2023).

options.text: Cyclical progesterone (medroxyprogesterone) for endometrial protection only
options.rationale: Cyclical progesterone provides endometrial protection but does not treat hirsutism or provide contraception; the COCP achieves both (Teede et al. 2023).

options.text: Clomiphene citrate to induce ovulation
options.rationale: Ovulation induction is offered when a woman is actively trying to conceive; she is not seeking pregnancy (Teede et al. 2023).

explanation.summary: Rotterdam criteria require any 2 of 3: oligo-anovulation, clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovarian morphology (>=20 follicles/ovary or volume >=10 mL) or elevated AMH. First-line management in non-fertility-seeking PCOS: lifestyle + COCP. Metformin is an adjunct, especially with BMI >25 or impaired glucose tolerance.
key_points: Rotterdam: 2/3 features after exclusion of mimics
Exclude mimics: TSH, prolactin, 17-OHP, beta-hCG; consider DHEAS if rapid virilisation
Within 8 years of menarche, do not use USS to make the diagnosis (PCOM common normally)
First-line non-fertility: lifestyle + COCP; metformin adjunct if metabolic risk
Long-term: screen IGT/T2DM 1-3 yearly, dyslipidaemia, endometrial protection, mental health, sleep apnoea
explanation.pearls: Spironolactone is teratogenic - pair with reliable contraception.

sources.label: Teede HJ et al. 2023 International PCOS Guideline

subtopic: Premature ovarian insufficiency - diagnosis and HRT
stem: A 33-year-old nulligravid woman attends her GP with 6 months of secondary amenorrhoea following a 12-month history of progressively irregular cycles. She reports new hot flushes (around 6-8 per day), troublesome night sweats with disturbed sleep, vaginal dryness with deep dyspareunia, low mood, reduced libido and increased fatigue. She is in a stable long-term relationship and is open to future fertility consideration, although they have not actively been trying to conceive. There is no recent significant weight change, no excessive exercise, no medication changes, no eating disorder behaviours, no galactorrhoea, no headaches or visual field symptoms. She has had no chemotherapy, no pelvic radiotherapy and no previous ovarian surgery. She had menarche at age 12 with previously regular 28-day cycles. Family history: her mother had menopause at age 41 and her maternal aunt had menopause in her early forties; one cousin has Hashimoto's thyroiditis. BMI 23. She is not using contraception currently. Beta-hCG is negative today. Initial bloods 5 weeks ago: FSH 56 IU/L, oestradiol low, prolactin normal, TSH normal, total testosterone normal. Today's repeat FSH and oestradiol have been collected and are pending.

data_table.Examination: BMI 23. No goitre. No visual field defect. Speculum: pale dry vaginal mucosa.
data_table.Investigations: Five weeks ago: FSH 56 IU/L (premenopausal <10), oestradiol low, LH 38, prolactin normal, TSH 1.8, beta-hCG negative. Today: bloods repeated, pending.

lead_in: If today's repeat FSH is similarly elevated (>25 IU/L), which is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Reassure and recheck FSH in 6 months as a single elevated FSH is non-diagnostic
options.rationale: Diagnosis of POI requires two elevated FSH levels (>25 IU/L) at least 4 weeks apart in a woman <40 with >=4 months of amenorrhoea; her first sample was already 5 weeks prior, and a confirmatory second sample establishes the diagnosis (ESHRE; Jean Hailes).

options.text: Commence the combined oral contraceptive pill solely for menopausal symptom control
options.rationale: While COCP can be used in POI for contraception plus oestrogen replacement, standard HRT provides more physiological replacement and better bone protection; either is acceptable but HRT is generally preferred unless contraception is required (ESHRE).

options.text: Bone density scan and a referral for fertility counselling, but defer hormonal therapy until age 40
options.rationale: Delaying oestrogen replacement until age 40 risks cardiovascular, bone and neurocognitive harm; oestrogen replacement should commence at diagnosis and be continued at least until the average age of natural menopause (~51) (ESHRE; Jean Hailes).

options.text: Refer to an endocrinologist for adrenal failure work-up before initiating any therapy
options.rationale: Adrenal autoimmune screen (21-hydroxylase antibodies) is part of the POI work-up but does not need to precede commencement of HRT; treatment should not be delayed (ESHRE).

options.text: Commence systemic HRT (oestradiol with cyclical or continuous progestogen) and offer fertility counselling, screen for adrenal/thyroid antibodies and karyotype, and arrange baseline bone densitometry
options.rationale: POI confirmed by FSH >25 IU/L on two occasions at least 4 weeks apart with amenorrhoea >=4 months in a woman <40 mandates HRT until at least the average age of natural menopause to protect bone and cardiovascular health; concurrent work-up includes karyotype, fragile X premutation, adrenal antibodies, thyroid antibodies, and a fertility discussion (ESHRE 2016; Jean Hailes).

explanation.summary: POI: amenorrhoea/oligomenorrhoea >=4 months + FSH >25 IU/L on 2 readings 4+ weeks apart, in a woman aged under 40. Work-up: karyotype, fragile X premutation, adrenal (21-OH) and thyroid antibodies, baseline DXA. Treat with HRT until at least the average age of natural menopause (~51) for bone, CV and neurocognitive protection. Spontaneous pregnancy possible in ~5-10%; HRT is not a contraceptive.
key_points: Diagnosis: amenorrhoea >=4 months + 2 x FSH >25 IU/L, 4+ weeks apart, age <40
Causes: idiopathic, genetic (Turner, fragile X), autoimmune, iatrogenic (chemo/radiotherapy/surgery)
HRT (or COCP) until ~age 51
Discuss contraception separately - HRT not contraceptive
Refer for fertility counselling early
explanation.pearls: POI is not just early menopause - women may still ovulate intermittently and conceive; HRT does not prevent pregnancy.

stem: A 22-year-old nulligravid woman presents to ED with sudden severe right iliac fossa pain that began 90 minutes ago while she was walking briskly home. The pain is constant, 9/10 in severity, sharp, with intermittent stabbing exacerbations, and is associated with two episodes of bilious vomiting and one episode of diarrhoea-free loose stool. She is sexually active with her regular partner of 18 months, uses condoms consistently, and her last menstrual period began 14 days ago and was normal in flow and duration. Her cycles are regular at 28 days and she has no abnormal vaginal bleeding, no postcoital bleeding and no abnormal discharge. There is no fever, no rigors, no dysuria, no frequency, no haematuria, no diarrhoea, no recent travel and no recent change in bowel habit. She had a similar but milder episode three months ago that resolved spontaneously after 4 hours; at that time she did not present for medical review. She had a similar mild episode 6 weeks ago that lasted 2 hours. She has no significant past medical history, no previous abdominal surgery, no allergies, and takes no regular medications. Her appendix has not been removed.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 108 bpm, T 36.9 degC, RR 18, SpO2 99% RA.
data_table.Examination: Pale and distressed. Abdomen: marked tenderness right iliac fossa with guarding but no rebound; bowel sounds present. Bimanual: marked right adnexal tenderness, cervical motion tenderness equivocal, no cervical discharge. No palpable adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative. FBC: WCC 12.1, neutrophils 8.9. CRP 8 mg/L. UEC normal. Urinalysis: trace blood, no nitrites, no leucocytes. Bedside USS in ED: enlarged right ovary 7 cm with a 5 cm complex cyst, minimal arterial Doppler flow within the ovary.

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy
options.rationale: A negative urine beta-hCG essentially excludes ectopic pregnancy; sensitivity of urine beta-hCG approaches 99% at the time symptoms develop (RANZCOG).

options.text: Ovarian torsion of a right ovarian cyst
options.rationale: Sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain with nausea/vomiting, an enlarged ovary with a cyst and reduced/absent arterial Doppler flow on TVUS in a haemodynamically stable woman with negative beta-hCG points to ovarian torsion; the prior self-resolving episode is typical of intermittent torsion (RANZCOG; BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Acute pelvic inflammatory disease
options.rationale: PID typically presents with bilateral lower abdominal pain, cervical motion tenderness, mucopurulent discharge, fever, and elevated inflammatory markers; the sudden onset, unilateral severity, normal CRP and absence of discharge here argue against PID (CDC; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Ruptured haemorrhagic ovarian cyst
options.rationale: A ruptured cyst typically produces sudden pain that improves over hours; USS shows free fluid in the pouch of Douglas without a persistent enlarged ovary and Doppler flow is preserved (BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice Ovarian cyst rupture

options.text: Acute appendicitis
options.rationale: Appendicitis usually progresses over 12-24 hours with central-to-RIF migration, anorexia and rising inflammatory markers; the abrupt very-severe onset, prior identical self-resolving episode, and TVUS findings of an enlarged ovary with reduced Doppler flow favour torsion (BMJ Best Practice).

explanation.summary: Ovarian torsion: sudden severe unilateral pain, nausea/vomiting, enlarged ovary +/- cyst on USS, reduced or absent Doppler flow (though preserved flow does not exclude torsion). Prior self-resolving episodes (intermittent torsion) are a major clue. Treatment is urgent laparoscopic detorsion with ovarian preservation.
key_points: Negative beta-hCG essentially excludes ectopic
Doppler flow: reduced/absent suggests torsion but normal flow does not exclude it (dual blood supply)
Ovarian mass >5 cm increases torsion risk
Treatment: urgent laparoscopy with detorsion; oophorectomy only if necrotic/malignant
Cystectomy of underlying cyst at the time, or interval if oedematous
explanation.pearls: Pregnancy, mass >5 cm, prior similar episodes, and right > left predominance are torsion risk hooks. Do not wait for absent Doppler flow before operating.

stem: A 21-year-old sexually active woman presents to a sexual health clinic with a 4-day history of lower abdominal pain, deep dyspareunia and mucopurulent vaginal discharge. She has had three sexual partners in the past 6 months and inconsistent condom use. She has no urinary frequency or dysuria, no fever, and no nausea or vomiting. On examination she has bilateral lower abdominal tenderness and cervical motion tenderness with a mucopurulent endocervical discharge. She is afebrile (T 37.1 degC), haemodynamically stable, not pregnant (urine beta-hCG negative), and tolerating oral intake. There is no palpable adnexal mass and no signs of tubo-ovarian abscess. A clinical diagnosis of mild-to-moderate pelvic inflammatory disease is made and outpatient empirical treatment is planned per Therapeutic Guidelines while awaiting endocervical NAAT for Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis, plus screening for syphilis, HIV and hepatitis B/C. She has no allergies and takes no regular medications. She is not pregnant and uses no contraception currently.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/76 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, T 37.1 degC.
data_table.Examination: Abdomen: bilateral lower abdominal tenderness. Bimanual: cervical motion tenderness, mucopurulent endocervical discharge, no palpable adnexal mass.

lead_in: What is the recommended empirical outpatient antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose plus doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 14 days plus metronidazole 400 mg orally twice daily for 14 days
options.rationale: This is the Australian Therapeutic Guidelines empirical regimen for mild-moderate PID, covering Neisseria gonorrhoeae (ceftriaxone), Chlamydia trachomatis and Mycoplasma genitalium (doxycycline) and anaerobes (metronidazole) (Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic 2023).

options.text: Amoxicillin 500 mg orally three times daily for 7 days
options.rationale: Amoxicillin monotherapy does not cover Neisseria gonorrhoeae or Chlamydia and has no role in PID (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Azithromycin 1 g orally as a single dose
options.rationale: Single-dose azithromycin is no longer recommended for chlamydia or PID due to rising macrolide resistance; doxycycline 7 days is the chlamydia treatment and 14 days for PID (Australian STI Management Guidelines).

options.text: Trimethoprim 300 mg orally daily for 3 days
options.rationale: Trimethoprim is for uncomplicated UTI; it does not cover the genital pathogens responsible for PID (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily plus gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV daily for 14 days
options.rationale: Parenteral therapy is for severe PID, tubo-ovarian abscess, pregnancy, sepsis, or inability to tolerate oral; this patient has mild-moderate disease and is suitable for outpatient oral therapy (Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Empirical outpatient PID regimen per Australian Therapeutic Guidelines: ceftriaxone 500 mg IM stat + doxycycline 100 mg BD for 14 days + metronidazole 400 mg BD for 14 days. Take partner notification, STI screen, and HIV/hepatitis screening at presentation.
key_points: Ceftriaxone covers gonorrhoea
Doxycycline covers chlamydia and M. genitalium (test/retest if treatment failure)
Metronidazole covers anaerobes
Admit and treat IV if pregnant, severe, TOA, sepsis, or failed oral therapy
Partner notification and contact tracing essential
explanation.pearls: PID is a clinical diagnosis - don't wait for swab results before starting antibiotics.

subtopic: Contraception - migraine with aura (UKMEC 4 for COCP)
stem: A 28-year-old nulligravid woman attends her GP for contraceptive counselling. She has been using condoms only since her last relationship ended and would now like a more reliable method as she is in a new monogamous relationship of 9 months. She has a 6-year history of migraine with aura: a visual scotoma with shimmering edge develops in one visual field for 20-30 minutes preceding a moderate-to-severe unilateral throbbing headache that lasts up to 24 hours. These attacks occur 1-2 times per month and are usually associated with sleep deprivation or menstruation. She has been reviewed by a neurologist who confirmed the diagnosis of migraine with aura. She is otherwise well, non-smoker, BMI 24, blood pressure 118/76 mmHg, and has no personal or family history of VTE, ischaemic heart disease or stroke. She has had no episodes of focal neurology between migraine attacks. She has not had children and is open to long-acting reversible methods. She would prefer a method she does not need to remember daily. She has no allergies and takes sumatriptan 50 mg as needed for acute migraine attacks; she is not on any prophylactic agent.

data_table.Examination: BP 118/76 mmHg. BMI 24. No focal neurology between attacks.
data_table.Investigations: None required for routine contraceptive counselling.

lead_in: Which contraceptive method is contraindicated in this patient?

options.text: Levonorgestrel 52 mg intrauterine system (Mirena)
options.rationale: The LNG-IUS is UKMEC category 1 in migraine with aura; the local progestogen action does not raise stroke risk (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Etonogestrel subdermal implant (Implanon NXT)
options.rationale: Progestogen-only implants are UKMEC category 2 in migraine with aura; benefits outweigh risks (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Copper intrauterine device
options.rationale: Non-hormonal Cu-IUD is UKMEC category 1 in migraine with aura (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill containing ethinyloestradiol 30 mcg and levonorgestrel 150 mcg
options.rationale: Combined hormonal contraception (COCP, patch, vaginal ring) is UKMEC category 4 (unacceptable risk) in migraine with aura due to a 2-4-fold increase in ischaemic stroke risk; absolute contraindication (FSRH UKMEC 2016; Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Progestogen-only pill (desogestrel 75 mcg)
options.rationale: POP is UKMEC category 2 in migraine with aura; benefits outweigh risks (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

explanation.summary: Migraine with aura is UKMEC category 4 for combined hormonal contraception (COCP, patch, ring) - an absolute contraindication. All progestogen-only methods (POP, implant, LNG-IUS, DMPA) and the copper IUD are safe (UKMEC 1-2). The increased stroke risk is driven by the oestrogen component.
key_points: Migraine WITH aura + CHC = UKMEC 4 (do not use)
Migraine WITHOUT aura + CHC = UKMEC 2 (initiation), 3 (continuation if aura develops)
All POP methods and IUDs are UKMEC 1-2 in migraine with aura
Smoking + age >=35 + COCP = also UKMEC 3-4
Aura = focal neurological symptoms preceding headache, typically 5-60 min
explanation.pearls: If migraines develop aura while on COCP, stop the COCP immediately and switch to a progestogen-only or non-hormonal method.

subtopic: Postnatal contraception in a breastfeeding woman
stem: A 31-year-old G2P2 woman attends her 6-week postnatal check with her local GP. She had an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal birth of a healthy term infant 6 weeks ago and is exclusively breastfeeding on demand, day and night, with no formula supplementation and no expressed-milk bottle feeds. The baby is feeding 8-12 times in 24 hours and weight gain is on track. She has not yet had a return of menses. She wishes to space pregnancies for at least 2 years and would prefer the most reliable method that is compatible with breastfeeding. She had mild gestational hypertension in the third trimester that resolved within 2 weeks postpartum, and her blood pressure today is 118/74 mmHg with no proteinuria. She has no history of VTE, no thrombophilia, no migraines and has a BMI of 25. She does not smoke. She is open to long-acting methods including intrauterine devices and the etonogestrel subdermal implant, and is also willing to consider the progestogen-only pill. She has not used the lactational amenorrhoea method as her sole strategy and is concerned about pregnancy. Her partner is not pursuing vasectomy in the near term. She has no contraindications to any specific contraceptive method on history.

data_table.Examination: Well. BP 118/74. BMI 25. Soft well-involuted uterus, no lochia, perineum healed. Lactating breasts, no mastitis.
data_table.Investigations: None required today; routine 6-week postnatal review.

lead_in: Regarding contraception, which statement is most accurate?

options.text: The combined oral contraceptive pill is the first-line choice as her blood pressure has normalised and gestational hypertension has resolved
options.rationale: Combined hormonal contraception is UKMEC category 4 (avoid) in breastfeeding women less than 6 weeks postpartum and category 2 between 6 weeks and 6 months due to potential impact on milk supply; progestogen-only methods are preferred while breastfeeding (FSRH; Family Planning Australia).

options.text: The lactational amenorrhoea method (LAM) is acceptable as a sole method indefinitely while she remains amenorrhoeic
options.rationale: LAM provides ~98% efficacy only if all three criteria are met: amenorrhoea, exclusive or near-exclusive breastfeeding, AND less than 6 months postpartum; reliability falls thereafter and she wants a more reliable method (Family Planning Australia).

options.text: A levonorgestrel intrauterine system or copper IUD can be inserted at this visit, and the etonogestrel implant or progestogen-only pill are also suitable
options.rationale: Intrauterine contraception (LNG-IUS or Cu-IUD) can be safely inserted from 4-6 weeks postpartum (UKMEC 1-2); progestogen-only methods (POP, implant, DMPA) are also suitable in breastfeeding women from any time postpartum (FSRH UKMEC; Family Planning Australia).

options.text: The etonogestrel implant should be delayed until 6 months postpartum because of an effect on milk supply
options.rationale: Progestogen-only implants do not adversely affect milk supply or infant outcomes and can be inserted at any time postpartum, including immediately postpartum (FSRH; WHO MEC).

options.text: Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate is contraindicated during breastfeeding
options.rationale: DMPA is UKMEC category 1 from 6 weeks postpartum in breastfeeding women (category 2 before 6 weeks) and is safe to use (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

explanation.summary: Postnatal contraception in breastfeeding women: progestogen-only methods (POP, implant, DMPA, LNG-IUS) are all safe at 6 weeks. Cu-IUD or LNG-IUS can be inserted from 4-6 weeks postpartum (or immediately postpartum within 48 h of birth). CHC is avoided <6 weeks (UKMEC 4) and category 2 between 6 weeks and 6 months while breastfeeding. LAM is ~98% effective only when ALL three criteria are met: <6 months, amenorrhoea, exclusive or near-exclusive breastfeeding.
key_points: POP can start day 21 postpartum without need to wait
IUD/IUS: insert immediately postpartum (within 48 h) or from 4-6 weeks
CHC: avoid <6 weeks if breastfeeding; from 6 weeks UKMEC 2; from 6 months UKMEC 1
DMPA: UKMEC 2 before 6 weeks if breastfeeding, then UKMEC 1
LAM strict criteria: amenorrhoea + exclusive BF + <6 months postpartum
explanation.pearls: Day 21 postpartum is the earliest fertility could return in non-breastfeeding women - that's why POP and CHC have day 21 as the earliest start if other criteria allow.

subtopic: Emergency contraception - efficacy ranking and timing
stem: A 25-year-old woman attends a sexual health clinic 90 hours after a single act of unprotected vaginal intercourse with her regular partner of 3 years. The condom broke and was noticed at the time. Her last menstrual period began 16 days ago and her usual cycle is 28 days; she is therefore around day 16, just after her likely ovulation date. She is not on any regular contraception currently and was previously using only condoms. She has no contraindications to copper or oral therapy, no chlamydia or gonorrhoea concerns (she had STI screening 4 months ago which was negative), and would accept any method including same-day intrauterine device placement if it is the most effective option. She is BMI 28, has no allergies, no regular medications, no enzyme-inducing drugs, no history of ectopic pregnancy and no abnormal genital tract anatomy. Her urine beta-hCG is negative today. She is keen to understand efficacy ranking so she can make an informed decision; she would also be interested in starting an ongoing contraceptive method at the same time if appropriate.
lead_in: Which statement best describes the comparative efficacy and timing windows for emergency contraception options available to her now?

options.text: Copper IUD is the most effective (within 5 days); ulipristal acetate is more effective than levonorgestrel especially with BMI >26 and is licensed up to 120 hours; oral levonorgestrel is licensed up to 72 hours (effective up to 96 hours but with declining efficacy)
options.rationale: Cu-IUD failure rate <1% within 5 days. UPA superior to LNG in the 72-120 hour window and around ovulation, and retains efficacy with raised BMI better than LNG. LNG 1.5 mg is licensed up to 72 hours; efficacy declines after this and BMI >26 reduces it further (Family Planning Australia; Cochrane).

options.text: Oral levonorgestrel is the most effective when given within 72 hours and is the first-line option in Australia
options.rationale: Cu-IUD is the most effective EC method; LNG efficacy is also reduced at her time interval (90 hours, outside the 72-hour licence) and at her BMI (Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Ulipristal acetate is contraindicated after 72 hours and at BMI >25
options.rationale: UPA is licensed up to 120 hours and retains better efficacy than LNG at higher BMI; it is not contraindicated at BMI 28 (Cochrane; FSRH).

options.text: The Yuzpe combined oral contraceptive regimen remains a first-line option due to its widespread availability
options.rationale: The Yuzpe regimen is less effective than LNG and causes more nausea; it is no longer recommended where other options are available (FSRH).

options.text: The levonorgestrel intrauterine system inserted within 5 days is equivalent to the copper IUD for emergency contraception
options.rationale: The LNG-IUS is not currently a recommended emergency contraceptive in Australian guidance; the Cu-IUD remains the IUD of choice (Family Planning Australia). Some international data are emerging but not yet endorsed in AU.

explanation.summary: EC efficacy ranking (most to least): Cu-IUD > ulipristal acetate > levonorgestrel > Yuzpe. Cu-IUD failure rate <1% and provides ongoing contraception for up to 10 years. UPA preferred at 72-120 hours and around ovulation; LNG less effective in this window. BMI >26 reduces oral EC efficacy.
key_points: Cu-IUD: most effective, within 5 days, ongoing contraception
UPA 30 mg: up to 120 hours, single dose, better than LNG near ovulation and at higher BMI
LNG 1.5 mg: licensed up to 72 hours, decreasing efficacy after
Avoid concurrent UPA and progestogen contraception for 5 days (delays UPA effect)
Offer Cu-IUD first to any woman who would accept it
explanation.pearls: Raised BMI shifts the balance toward Cu-IUD and away from oral EC. Time since intercourse and stage of cycle matter.

subtopic: Cervical screening - oncogenic non-16/18 HPV management
stem: A 38-year-old woman has her routine 5-yearly cervical screening test through her general practice. She is not pregnant, has had two previous CSTs at ages 28 and 33 which were both negative for oncogenic HPV, and has had no abnormal vaginal bleeding, no postcoital bleeding, no intermenstrual bleeding and no pelvic pain. She is in a stable monogamous relationship of 11 years and uses the LNG-IUS 52 mg (Mirena, inserted 4 years ago for both contraception and HMB control) and is currently amenorrhoeic. She has had a total of 4 lifetime sexual partners and has never had a sexually transmitted infection identified. She received the quadrivalent HPV vaccine series at age 14 through the school-based program. Her practitioner-collected sample returns: oncogenic HPV detected (non-16/18 type); reflex liquid-based cytology performed automatically by the laboratory shows no intraepithelial lesion or malignancy. She is immunocompetent and has no history of cervical intraepithelial neoplasia or cervical excisional treatment. She does not smoke.

data_table.Examination: Routine speculum normal at time of collection.
data_table.Investigations: HPV test: oncogenic non-16/18 detected. Reflex LBC: negative for intraepithelial lesion or malignancy.

lead_in: Under the Australian National Cervical Screening Program, what is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Direct referral for colposcopy with biopsy
options.rationale: Direct colposcopy is reserved for HPV 16/18 detection regardless of cytology, or for any oncogenic HPV with possible high-grade or higher cytology, or persistent HPV at 24 months (NCSP 2022).

options.text: Repeat HPV testing in 12 months (intermediate-risk pathway)
options.rationale: Oncogenic non-16/18 HPV with reflex LBC negative or low-grade abnormality places the patient in the intermediate-risk group; repeat HPV at 12 months. If still HPV-positive then refer to colposcopy; if negative, return to 5-yearly screening (NCSP 2022).

options.text: Return to routine 5-yearly screening
options.rationale: 5-yearly screening resumes only after HPV becomes negative; any oncogenic HPV detection triggers the 12-month repeat or colposcopy pathway (NCSP 2022).

options.text: Repeat HPV test in 6 months
options.rationale: 6-month repeat is not the NCSP-recommended interval; intermediate-risk repeat is at 12 months (NCSP 2022).

options.text: Refer for excisional treatment (LLETZ) without further investigation
options.rationale: Treatment is based on histological diagnosis of high-grade dysplasia from colposcopy/biopsy, not on HPV status with negative cytology (NCSP 2022).

explanation.summary: Under the NCSP: HPV 16/18 detected -> direct colposcopy regardless of LBC. Oncogenic HPV non-16/18 with negative or low-grade reflex LBC -> repeat HPV in 12 months (intermediate risk). Oncogenic HPV non-16/18 with possible high-grade or worse on LBC -> colposcopy. Persistent oncogenic HPV at 24 months -> colposcopy.
key_points: HPV 16/18 (any LBC): direct colposcopy
Other oncogenic HPV + negative/low-grade LBC: repeat HPV at 12 months
Other oncogenic HPV + possible high-grade or worse LBC: colposcopy
If 12-month repeat HPV negative -> return to 5-yearly screening
Self-collected vaginal swab is now universally available since July 2022
explanation.pearls: Reflex LBC is performed automatically by the lab if oncogenic HPV is detected; the practitioner does not need to order it separately.

subtopic: Postmenopausal bleeding - TVUS endometrial thickness threshold
stem: A 62-year-old G3P3 woman presents to her general practice with a 6-week history of intermittent painless vaginal bleeding. She has been menopausal since age 51 (last menstrual period at 51) and has had no episodes of bleeding in the intervening 11 years until now. The current bleeding is light to moderate, requires a pad most days, and has been ongoing intermittently for the last 6 weeks. There is no associated abdominal or pelvic pain, no urinary symptoms, no bowel change and no weight loss. She has never been on menopausal hormone therapy, has never used tamoxifen, and has no family history of endometrial, ovarian, breast or bowel cancer (specifically no features of Lynch syndrome on family history). Her BMI is 33, she has type 2 diabetes managed with metformin 1 g twice daily (HbA1c 6.6%), and her hypertension is well controlled on perindopril 5 mg daily. She has had three previous spontaneous vaginal births, none complicated, and no postpartum haemorrhage. Her last cervical screening test was negative for oncogenic HPV 18 months ago. She is on no anticoagulants and no antiplatelets. A transvaginal ultrasound is being arranged today.

data_table.Examination: BMI 33. Speculum: small amount of dark blood at cervix, no visible cervical lesion. Bimanual: uterus mobile, normal size, no adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 122 g/L, platelets 240. UEC and LFTs normal. Beta-hCG not indicated. Cervical screening negative 18 months ago.

lead_in: Regarding TVUS endometrial thickness in this clinical setting, which statement is most accurate?

options.text: If TVUS endometrial thickness is <=2 mm, endometrial sampling is mandatory regardless
options.rationale: Endometrial thickness <=4 mm has a very low risk of endometrial cancer and TVUS alone may be sufficient as initial triage in low-risk women; sampling is not mandatory below this cut-off if bleeding does not recur (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: A TVUS endometrial thickness of 3 mm essentially excludes endometrial cancer and no further investigation is needed even if bleeding recurs
options.rationale: Although a thin endometrium has a high negative predictive value, recurrent or persistent PMB requires endometrial sampling (Pipelle +/- hysteroscopy) regardless of TVUS thickness (RCOG).

options.text: Tamoxifen use makes TVUS thickness measurement unreliable due to subepithelial cysts; saline-infusion sonohysterography or hysteroscopy is preferred
options.rationale: True but not directly relevant - she is not on tamoxifen. The principle is correct but does not best describe her current management (RANZCOG).

options.text: She should proceed directly to hysteroscopy without TVUS, given her risk factors
options.rationale: TVUS is the appropriate first-line imaging; hysteroscopy is offered for thickened endometrium >=4 mm, recurrent PMB, or non-diagnostic Pipelle (RCOG; RANZCOG).

options.text: An endometrial thickness >=4 mm on TVUS warrants endometrial sampling (Pipelle biopsy) with hysteroscopy if focal lesions are seen or sampling is non-diagnostic
options.rationale: The widely used 4 mm cut-off has a sensitivity of ~95% for endometrial carcinoma in postmenopausal bleeding; endometrial sampling is indicated above this threshold, with hysteroscopy +/- directed biopsy if focal pathology is suspected or Pipelle is non-diagnostic (RCOG Green-top 67; RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: TVUS endometrial thickness <=4 mm has a high negative predictive value (~99%) for endometrial cancer in PMB; sampling not mandatory if bleeding is a single episode. Thickness >=4 mm triggers Pipelle biopsy +/- hysteroscopy. Recurrent PMB warrants sampling regardless of TVUS. Risk factors: obesity, T2DM, nulliparity, late menopause, unopposed oestrogen, tamoxifen, Lynch syndrome.
key_points: TVUS endometrial thickness >=4 mm -> Pipelle biopsy
Recurrent PMB -> sample regardless of TVUS thickness
Hysteroscopy if focal lesion, non-diagnostic Pipelle, or thickened endometrium
Risk factors: obesity, DM, PCOS, nulliparity, tamoxifen, Lynch, unopposed oestrogen
Approximately 10% of PMB has endometrial cancer
explanation.pearls: Any PMB warrants investigation. A thin endometrium does not equal a benign outcome if bleeding recurs.

stem: A 53-year-old G2P2 woman presents to her GP with 18 months of disabling vasomotor symptoms: 8-12 hot flushes per day with drenching night sweats requiring change of nightwear most nights, fragmented sleep with average 4-5 hours, persistently low mood (PHQ-9 score 11), and reduced libido. She has tried fan, cooling clothing, regular exercise, alcohol avoidance and trigger-diary approaches with limited benefit. Her last menstrual period was 14 months ago and she is therefore postmenopausal by clinical criteria. She has an intact uterus and has had no episodes of postmenopausal bleeding. Her BMI is 31, BP 132/84 mmHg, fasting lipids in target range and HbA1c 5.5%. She had a single provoked DVT after a long-haul flight 8 years ago and was anticoagulated for 3 months; no thrombophilia was identified and she has had no subsequent VTE. She has no personal or family history of breast cancer, no ischaemic heart disease, and no migraine with aura. She does not smoke. Cervical screening and mammography are up to date and normal. She has trialled citalopram 20 mg daily for 4 months with minimal symptomatic benefit and is now seeking menopausal hormone therapy. She does not want a monthly bleed and would prefer a no-bleed regimen if possible.

data_table.Examination: BMI 31. BP 132/84. Breast examination normal. Pelvic examination unremarkable.
data_table.Investigations: Fasting lipids: total cholesterol 5.4 mmol/L, LDL 3.1. HbA1c 5.5%. TSH 1.6 mIU/L. Mammogram normal 10 months ago.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate MHT regimen for her?

options.text: Oral oestradiol 1 mg daily plus cyclical oral medroxyprogesterone acetate 10 mg for 12 days each month
options.rationale: Cyclical progestogen produces a planned monthly bleed and is preferred in perimenopausal women still having periods; this woman is >12 months postmenopausal and prefers a no-bleed regimen, so continuous combined therapy is preferred. Additionally, oral oestrogen is best avoided given her prior VTE (BMS; Jean Hailes).

options.text: Oral conjugated equine oestrogen 0.625 mg daily plus oral medroxyprogesterone acetate 2.5 mg continuously
options.rationale: Oral oestrogen increases VTE risk (~2-fold); transdermal oestrogen at standard doses does not increase VTE risk and is preferred in women with prior VTE, obesity, or other VTE risk (BMS; Jean Hailes).

options.text: Tibolone 2.5 mg daily as monotherapy
options.rationale: Tibolone is a reasonable alternative for vasomotor and libido symptoms in postmenopausal women, but it carries an increased stroke risk (especially age >60) and is not first-line in a woman with elevated cardiometabolic risk and prior VTE (BMS).

options.text: Transdermal oestradiol patch 50 mcg/24 h twice weekly plus levonorgestrel 52 mg intrauterine system (Mirena) for endometrial protection
options.rationale: Transdermal oestrogen avoids first-pass hepatic metabolism and does not increase VTE risk at standard doses, making it the preferred route in women with prior VTE, obesity or other VTE risk factors. The LNG-IUS provides effective endometrial protection in a no-bleed manner. Together this provides combined MHT for a woman with an intact uterus (BMS 2024; Jean Hailes).

options.text: Transdermal oestradiol patch 50 mcg/24 h twice weekly alone (no progestogen)
options.rationale: Unopposed oestrogen in a woman with an intact uterus causes endometrial hyperplasia and increases endometrial cancer risk; a progestogen (oral, transdermal, vaginal, or LNG-IUS) is mandatory (BMS; Jean Hailes).

explanation.summary: MHT principles: oestrogen alone if hysterectomy; combined (oestrogen + progestogen) if uterus intact. Cyclical progestogen for perimenopausal women still having periods; continuous combined for women >=12 months postmenopausal who want no bleed. Transdermal oestrogen preferred when VTE risk is elevated (prior VTE, obesity, age) - no first-pass hepatic effect. LNG-IUS is an effective progestogen for endometrial protection.
key_points: Uterus intact -> add progestogen (mandatory)
Hysterectomy -> oestrogen alone
Cyclical progestogen if still cycling; continuous if >12 months amenorrhoea
Transdermal oestrogen: no increased VTE risk; preferred if VTE risk factors
Absolute contraindications: current/recent breast cancer, oestrogen-dependent malignancy, undiagnosed PV bleeding, active VTE/PE, active liver disease, untreated endometrial hyperplasia
explanation.pearls: If a woman has a uterus and you forget the progestogen, you cause endometrial cancer. LNG-IUS plus transdermal oestrogen is an elegant solution in many women.

subtopic: Genitourinary syndrome of menopause - local oestrogen
stem: A 67-year-old woman presents to her GP with 18 months of progressive vaginal dryness, deep dyspareunia and urinary urgency without incontinence. She has had three episodes of culture-confirmed E. coli urinary tract infection in the past 12 months, each requiring antibiotics, with no upper-tract involvement. She had a left-sided early-stage T1N0 oestrogen-receptor-positive breast cancer treated with breast-conserving lumpectomy, sentinel node biopsy, adjuvant radiotherapy and 5 years of adjuvant tamoxifen completed 12 months ago. She did not receive an aromatase inhibitor. She is currently in remission with no evidence of disease on her most recent annual surveillance mammogram and clinical breast examination 4 months ago. She is not on systemic MHT. She has tried over-the-counter water-based vaginal lubricants for intercourse and a non-hormonal hyaluronic acid-based vaginal moisturiser (Replens) used three times weekly consistently for 4 months with only modest symptomatic benefit. She is sexually active with her long-term partner and her quality of life and intimate relationship are significantly affected. Her treating breast oncologist is aware she is exploring further treatment options for her genitourinary symptoms and is supportive of considering local therapies after shared decision-making. She has no other oestrogen-dependent malignancy and no undiagnosed bleeding.

data_table.Examination: Speculum: pale thin atrophic vaginal mucosa, friable, loss of rugae. No discharge, no lesions. Bimanual: normal-sized uterus, no adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Urine MCS today: no growth. Last surveillance mammogram and clinical breast examination 4 months ago: no evidence of disease.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Avoid all oestrogen-containing therapies indefinitely and continue non-hormonal moisturisers and lubricants only
options.rationale: While historically avoided, current evidence shows low-dose vaginal oestrogen produces minimal systemic absorption and recent observational data suggest no increased breast cancer recurrence risk in most survivors; ongoing severe GSM warrants further options after shared decision-making with the breast oncologist (BMS; Jean Hailes; NAMS).

options.text: Commence systemic oestradiol patch 50 mcg/24 h plus an LNG-IUS for endometrial protection
options.rationale: Systemic MHT is generally avoided in breast cancer survivors and is not required for GSM; local low-dose vaginal oestrogen is more appropriate and effective (BMS; Jean Hailes).

options.text: Trial of low-dose vaginal oestriol cream or estradiol pessary in consultation with her breast oncologist, alongside continued use of non-hormonal moisturisers and lubricants
options.rationale: Local vaginal oestrogen in a breast cancer survivor not on aromatase inhibitors is appropriate after shared decision-making with the oncology team; severe GSM significantly affects quality of life and the systemic absorption is minimal. Current evidence does not show increased recurrence risk for most survivors. She is on no current endocrine therapy (completed tamoxifen 12 months ago) (BMS 2024; Jean Hailes; NAMS 2020).

options.text: Refer for laser (CO2) vaginal treatment as a first-line alternative
options.rationale: Vaginal laser therapy lacks high-quality evidence of efficacy and safety; the TGA and international societies have cautioned against its use for GSM outside clinical trials (TGA; NAMS).

options.text: Commence ospemifene as the first-line therapy for her dyspareunia
options.rationale: Ospemifene is a selective oestrogen receptor modulator licensed for dyspareunia, but it is contraindicated in current/recent breast cancer due to oestrogen agonist effects on breast tissue (FDA labelling; BMS).

explanation.summary: GSM (vaginal atrophy + urinary symptoms) responds well to low-dose vaginal oestrogen with minimal systemic absorption. In breast cancer survivors, local vaginal oestrogen is increasingly used after shared decision-making with the oncologist, especially in those not on aromatase inhibitors and with significant symptom burden. Non-hormonal moisturisers and lubricants are first-line; vaginal oestrogen is added when symptoms persist.
key_points: First-line: non-hormonal moisturisers (Replens) + lubricants
Second-line: low-dose vaginal oestriol cream or oestradiol pessary
Local vaginal oestrogen: minimal systemic absorption
In breast cancer survivors: shared decision with oncology; tamoxifen users generally acceptable; AI users more contentious
GSM also reduces recurrent UTIs in postmenopausal women
explanation.pearls: Recurrent UTIs in a postmenopausal woman are often a manifestation of GSM and respond to vaginal oestrogen better than to recurrent antibiotic courses.

sources.label: Jean Hailes: Genitourinary syndrome of menopause

subtopic: Lichen sclerosus - surveillance and malignancy risk
stem: A 64-year-old G2P2 postmenopausal woman has a 6-year established diagnosis of vulval lichen sclerosus, biopsy-confirmed at the time of diagnosis. She has been managed with topical clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment using a structured regimen (nightly for 4 weeks, then alternate nights for 4 weeks, then twice weekly maintenance long-term) and has been reasonably symptom-controlled until recently, with annual review by her gynaecologist. Over the past 4 months she has noticed a persistent fissured area on the right labium majus that has not healed despite optimisation of her steroid technique (correct application, adequate quantity confirmed at recent review). Symptoms include localised pruritus that has worsened, occasional spotting when wiping after micturition, and increasing discomfort with intercourse despite use of personal lubricant. She has no other rashes, no oral lichen planus, no eye symptoms, no skin lesions elsewhere, and no urinary or bowel symptoms. She has no postcoital bleeding aside from the localised spotting described. She is on no immunosuppressive medications and is otherwise well, with controlled hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily. Her long-term partner has no penile lesions or symptoms. There is no history of previous vulval intraepithelial neoplasia, no previous vulval surgery, and her last cervical screening 18 months ago was negative for oncogenic HPV. She does not smoke. She wonders whether she still needs ongoing specialist follow-up after 6 years of stable disease, or whether she could now safely self-manage with intermittent steroid use only as needed.

data_table.Examination: Vulva: classic white atrophic 'cigarette paper' skin extending in a figure-of-eight around vulva and perianal area, loss of labia minora architecture. New finding: 1.2 cm hyperkeratotic, fissured, indurated plaque on right labium majus that has not healed.
data_table.Investigations: None done today; review appointment for chronic LS surveillance.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Increase topical clobetasol propionate to nightly application for a further 8 weeks and review symptoms
options.rationale: An unhealed, indurated or hyperkeratotic lesion in a woman with longstanding lichen sclerosus must not be assumed to be a steroid-responsive feature; it requires biopsy to exclude differentiated VIN or invasive squamous cell carcinoma (BSSVD; RCOG).

options.text: Add topical tacrolimus 0.1% ointment as a second-line steroid-sparing agent
options.rationale: Topical calcineurin inhibitors are a recognised second-line agent in lichen sclerosus, but the priority here is to biopsy the persistent suspicious lesion before changing therapy (BSSVD).

options.text: Reassure that surveillance is not required after 5 years of stable disease and discharge to self-management with PRN steroid as needed
options.rationale: Lichen sclerosus carries a lifetime risk of vulval squamous cell carcinoma of approximately 4-5%; long-term clinician surveillance is recommended at least annually with prompt biopsy of any persistent change (BSSVD; RCOG).

options.text: Refer for wide local excision of the suspicious lesion without prior histology
options.rationale: Diagnostic biopsy precedes any consideration of wide local excision; excisional treatment depends on the histological diagnosis (BSSVD; RCOG).

options.text: Refer urgently to gynaecology/vulval clinic for punch biopsy of the persistent fissured lesion, continue topical clobetasol, and plan ongoing at least annual surveillance
options.rationale: Persistent ulcer, hyperkeratotic plaque, induration, or non-healing lesion in established lichen sclerosus requires prompt biopsy to exclude differentiated VIN (the precursor most associated with LS-related vulval SCC, typically HPV-negative) and invasive SCC. Long-term annual surveillance is recommended due to ~4-5% lifetime risk of vulval SCC. Topical potent steroid (clobetasol) remains the cornerstone of medical management (BSSVD 2018; RCOG; Cancer Council).

explanation.summary: Vulval lichen sclerosus is a chronic inflammatory dermatosis with a 4-5% lifetime risk of vulval squamous cell carcinoma (via differentiated VIN, typically HPV-negative). Management: potent topical corticosteroid (clobetasol 0.05% ointment) induction then maintenance, plus long-term clinician surveillance. Any persistent ulcer, hyperkeratotic plaque, induration or non-healing lesion warrants prompt biopsy. Self-management alone is inappropriate due to malignancy risk.
key_points: Clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment is the gold-standard topical therapy
Structured induction then maintenance (typically twice weekly long-term)
Lifetime vulval SCC risk ~4-5% via differentiated VIN (HPV-negative pathway)
Biopsy: persistent ulcer, hyperkeratotic plaque, induration, non-healing lesion
At least annual clinician review long-term, more frequent if uncontrolled or atypical features
Tacrolimus second-line; surgery reserved for adhesions/scarring or malignancy
explanation.pearls: Differentiated VIN looks innocuous and is easy to miss - any unhealed area in LS deserves a biopsy regardless of how unremarkable it appears.

sources.label: RCOG: The management of vulval skin disorders
